items.fix: Each test sets up and tears down its own state. Use fixtures with proper scope.
items.why: Order-dependent tests pass solo and fail in CI (or vice versa) — flaky and untrustworthy.

items.mistake: Hitting the network / real LLM in a 'unit' test.
items.fix: Mock the API client. Reserve real-API tests for a separate, opt-in integration suite.
items.why: Network tests are slow, flaky, cost money (token bills), and fail when the API is down.
items.example:
```python
from unittest.mock import patch

@patch('ragapp.llm.client.chat.completions.create')
def test_summarize_calls_llm(mock_create):
    mock_create.return_value.choices[0].message.content = 'summary'
    assert summarize('long text') == 'summary'
```

items.mistake: One giant test that asserts 15 things.
items.fix: Split into focused tests, each with one clear behaviour and one failure message.
items.why: When a giant test fails you only learn the *first* failed assertion; the rest hides.

items.mistake: Asserting on the implementation, not the behaviour.
items.fix: Assert what the caller observes; let internals refactor freely.
items.why: Implementation-coupled tests break on every harmless refactor — tests become a tax instead of an asset.
items.example: Don't assert `chunker._buffer == [...]`. Assert that `chunker.chunks(...)` returns the expected list.

items.mistake: Catching exceptions in the test body instead of using `pytest.raises`.
items.fix: Use `with pytest.raises(ExpectedError):` — fails the test if the exception isn't raised.
items.why: A bare try/except silently passes when the exception you expected didn't happen.
items.example:
```python
# BAD
try:
    chunk_text("hi", -1)
except ValueError:
    pass   # silently passes even if no error raised!

# GOOD
with pytest.raises(ValueError):
    chunk_text("hi", -1)
```
